[Australia national cricket team]
On 25 March 2018, during the third Test match against hosts South Africa; players Cameron Bancroft, Steve Smith, David Warner and the leadership group of the team were implicated in a ball tampering scandal. Smith and Bancroft admitted to conspiring to alter the condition of the ball by rubbing it with a piece of adhesive tape containing abrasive granules picked up from the ground (it was later revealed that sandpaper was used). Smith stated that the purpose was to gain an advantage by unlawfully changing the ball's surface in order to generate reverse swing. Bancroft had been filmed tampering with the ball and, after being informed he had been caught, he was seen to transfer a yellow object from a pocket to the inside front of his trousers to hide the evidence. Steve Smith and David Warner were stood down as captain and vice-captain during the third Test while head coach, Darren Lehmann was suspected to have assisted Cameron Bancroft to tamper the ball. 
The ICC imposed a one-match ban and 100%-match-fee fine on Smith, while Bancroft was fined 75 percent of his match fee and received 3 demerit points. Smith and Warner were both stripped of their captaincy roles by Cricket Australia and sent home from the tour (along with Bancroft). Tim Paine was appointed as captain for the fourth Test. Context:
[Chennai Super Kings]
Telecom service provider Aircel became the first shirt sponsor for the team after it signed a three-year deal in 2008. The deal was renewed in 2011 for ₹850 million (US$8.9 million), then the most expensive sponsorship deal in the IPL. Muthoot Group was the principal shirt sponsor from 2018 to 2020 after which Myntra took over as the principal shirt sponsor for the 2021 season.
In 2022, tyre maker Eurogrip Tyres signed up with the franchise as the principal shirt sponsor on a three-year deal worth over ₹1 billion (US$10 million). Etihad Airways signed as one of the principal sponsors ahead of 2024 season with the brand displayed on the backside of the jersey. Ahead of the 2025 season, Etihad became the primary shirt sponsor, and the franchise signed a multi-year deal with FedEx, by which the company became the official logistics partner and the logo placed in the back of the shirts. The team also has sponsorship deals with India Cements, Gulf Oil, SNJ Breweries, Reliance Jio, Astral Pipes, ICICI Bank, Coca-Cola and Vision11.
Reebok, owned by German brand Adidas, was the official kit supplier for the Super Kings from 2008 to 2014. From the 2015 season, Australian apparel and sports gear manufacturer Spartan manufactured kits for the team. Indian apparel brand Seven became the official merchandising partner of the Super Kings in 2018. Chennai Super Kings is registered as a brand and sells cricket kits, apparel and other merchandise.

[Chennai Super Kings]
The Chennai Super Kings, also known as CSK, are a professional Twenty20 cricket team based in Chennai, Tamil Nadu, that competes in the Indian Premier League (IPL). The team was one of the eight debut franchises when the league was established in 2008. The team plays its home matches at the M. A. Chidambaram Stadium and is owned by Chennai Super Kings Cricket.
The Super Kings are the joint-most successful IPL franchise, along with Mumbai Indians, having won five IPL titles each. It has also appeared in ten finals and qualified for the playoff stages 12 times, the most amongst the IPL teams. The franchise has also won the Champions League Twenty20 twice in 2010 and 2014. The team is currently captained by Ruturaj Gaikwad.
The Super Kings were suspended for two years from the IPL from July 2015 to 2017 due to the involvement of its owners in the 2013 IPL spot-fixing and betting case. The franchise re-joined the tournament for the 2018 season and won the title in its comeback season. In January 2022, CSK became India's first unicorn sports enterprise. As of 2022, it was the second most valuable IPL franchise with a valuation of $1.15 billion.

== History ==

[Chennai Super Kings]
== Ownership and finances ==
When the IPL was launched in 2008, the Chennai franchise was sold to the India Cements for $91 million, making it the fourth most expensive team in the league. N. Srinivasan, who was then the treasurer and vice-chairman of the BCCI, became the de facto owner of the Super Kings as the managing director of India Cements. In September 2008, former BCCI President A. C. Muthiah wrote to then BCCI President citing the BCCI regulations, which prevented any administrator from holding commercial interests in the matches conducted by the board. Subsequently, the regulations were amended by the BCCI to exclude the IPL and a case was filed against the same in the Madras High Court. In 2013, he Muthiah moved to the Supreme Court to stop Srinivasan from taking over as the President of BCCI, which was rejected and Srinivasan was elected as the President of BCCI. In a subsequent decision announced on 22 January 2015, the Supreme Court stuck down the 2008 amendment to the BCCI constitution that allowed board officials to have a commercial interest in the IPL. As a result, the Super Kings franchise was transferred to a separate entity named Chennai Super Kings Cricket Limited.
As of 2021, about 30% of the franchise is owned by the trustees and shareholders of India Cements. The Super Kings recorded revenues of ₹4.18 billion (US$44 million) with a net profit of ₹1.11 billion (US$12 million) for FY 2018–19. In the FY 2020–21, the revenue dropped to ₹2.54 billion (US$

== Background ==
On 14 July 2015, the RM Lodha committee suspended the owners of the Chennai Super Kings and Rajasthan Royals franchises for a period of two years on charges of spot-fixing and betting during the 2013 IPL season. This meant that the two teams could not play in the 2016 and 2017 IPL seasons. The Board of Control of Cricket in India (BCCI) confirmed that two new teams will replace them for the next two seasons of the IPL.
In October 2015, PepsiCo withdrew as the title sponsor of the IPL, terminating a five-year deal which was to end in 2017. Chinese smartphone manufacturer Vivo was awarded the title sponsorship for the 2016 and 2017 seasons.
In November 2015, the BCCI shortlisted nine cities for the new franchises to be based in, leaving out Jaipur (home of Rajasthan Royals) and Kochi (home of the now defunct Kochi Tuskers Kerala) for unknown reasons. The nine cities shortlisted were: Chennai, Cuttack, Dharamshala, Indore, Nagpur, Pune, Rajkot, Ranchi and Visakhapatnam. The new franchises were allocated using a reverse auction process, with companies which bid the lowest share of the central revenue pool becoming the owners of the new teams. On 3 December, it was reported that twelve companies collected tender documents for the bidding process.

Context:
[Jonathan Agnew]
=== Personality ===
During Agnew's playing career, a dispute with team-mate Phillip DeFreitas attracted media attention: when DeFreitas poured salt over Agnew's lunch, Agnew responded by throwing DeFreitas' cricket bag and kit from the dressing room balcony. Former England cricketer Derek Pringle has written about Agnew's sense of humour, describing him as "hysterical". The pair toured Sri Lanka together on England B's 1986 tour. Pringle recalls that one hot day when England were in the field, Agnew came in for lunch: "It's fucking red hot on the field, and when you come off it's fucking red hot in the dressing room," Agnew screamed. "Then, what do you get for lunch, fucking red hot curry?"

=== Assisted suicide allegation ===
It was reported in various newspapers in 2013 that Agnew had offered to accompany Brian Dodds, his second wife's ex-husband, to the Dignitas assisted suicide clinic in Zürich after Dodds was diagnosed with motor neurone disease. Dodds died in England from the disease in 2005.

== Bibliography ==
Agnew has written four books:

8 Days a Week: Diary of a Professional Cricketer. Ringpress. 1988. ISBN 0-948955-30-9.
Over to You, Aggers. Gollancz. 1997. ISBN 0-575-06454-4.
Thanks, Johnners: An Affectionate Tribute to a Broadcasting Legend. HarperCollins. 2010. ISBN 978-0-00-734309-6.
Aggers' Ashes. HarperCollins. 2011. ISBN 978-0-00-734312-6.

== Notes ==

== References ==

[Jonathan Agnew]
Jonathan Philip Agnew (born 4 April 1960) is an English cricket broadcaster and a former cricketer. He was born in Macclesfield, Cheshire, and educated at Uppingham School. He is nicknamed "Aggers" and, less commonly, "Spiro" – the latter, according to Debrett's Cricketers' Who's Who, after former US Vice-President Spiro Agnew.
Agnew had a first-class career as a fast bowler for Leicestershire from 1979 to 1990, returning briefly in 1992. In first-class cricket he took 666 wickets at an average of 29.25.  Agnew won three Test caps for England, as well as playing three One Day Internationals in the mid-1980s, although his entire international career lasted just under a year. In county cricket, Agnew's most successful seasons came toward the end of his career when he had learned to swing the ball. He was second- and third-leading wicket-taker in 1987 and 1988 respectively, including the achievement of 100 wickets in a season in 1987. He was named as one of the five Cricketers of the Year by Wisden Cricketers' Almanack in 1988.
While still a player, Agnew began a career in cricket journalism and commentary. Since his retirement as a player, he has become a leading voice of cricket on radio, as the BBC Radio cricket correspondent and as a commentator on Test Match Special.  He has also contributed as a member of Australian broadcaster Australian Broadcasting Corporation's Grandstand team.
Agnew's on-air "leg over" comment on Test Match Special, made to fellow commentator Brian Jo

[Jonathan Agnew]
"For an eighteen-year-old bowler I was unusually fast, and enjoyed terrorising our opponents, be they schoolboys (8 wickets for 2 runs and 7 for 11 stick in the memory) or, better still, the teachers in the annual staff match. This, I gather, used to be a friendly affair until I turned up, and I relished the chance to settle a few scores on behalf of my friends – for whom I was the equivalent of a hired assassin – as well as for myself."
Having played for Surrey under-19s the previous year, he began playing for Surrey's second XI in 1977, but Surrey made no move to sign him as a player. At a home match against Hampshire, the teenage Agnew was the only player to stand up to then Surrey coach and former England player Fred Titmus after the latter racially abused the Guyanese-born Surrey player Lonsdale Skinner, an incident of which Agnew later said: "The consequences hadn’t really dawned on me. But clearly it was a career-ender". Leicestershire County Cricket Club did, however, take note of Agnew's impressive performances in local club cricket and for Uppingham School, for whom he took 37 wickets at a bowling average of 8 in 1977, and signed him while he was still a schoolboy in time for the 1978 season.

[Jonathan Agnew]
== Playing career ==

=== Background and early years ===
Agnew was born on 4 April 1960 at West Park Hospital in Macclesfield, Cheshire, to Margaret (née McConnell) and Philip Agnew. His parents' forthcoming marriage was announced in The Times in 1957: Philip Agnew was described as "the only son of Mr and Mrs Norris M. Agnew of Dukenfield Hall, Mobberley, Cheshire" and Margaret as "youngest daughter of Mr and Mrs A.F.V. McConnell of Hampton Hall, Worthen, Shropshire". The Agnews had a second son in June 1962 and were recorded as living at "Bainton near Stamford, Lincs"; in April 1966, a daughter, Felicity, was born and was announced as "a sister for Jonathan and Christopher". Agnew's paternal grandmother, Lady Mona Agnew, died aged 110 years and 170 days in 2010 and was on the list of the 100 longest-lived British people ever.
Jonathan Agnew recalls growing up on the family farm and first becoming aware of cricket aged "eight or nine"; his father would carry a radio around and listen to Test Match Special:

[Jonathan Agnew]
=== BBC reprimand ===
In April 2019, journalist Jonathan Liew wrote an article in which he criticised the media response to Jofra Archer’s selection for England, noting as an example how Agnew had expressed concern over the potential effect of Archer's inclusion on the team's "morale and camaraderie" but had welcomed the selection of white foreign-born players such as Gary Ballance; Liew stated that "there’s an incendiary word you could posit to describe all this, but I’m not going to use it." In response, Agnew sent several abusive direct messages to Liew on Twitter, describing him as "Fucking disgraceful. You have massive chips on your shoulder ... you are a racist," going on to demand an apology, calling him a "cunt" and asking "who the fuck are you?" Liew reposted the DMs publicly on his Twitter account. Agnew was reprimanded by the BBC for the messages, and issued an apology to Liew; he also resigned from the Cricket Writers' Club. Writing about the incident, journalist Barney Ronay stated, "Agnew has done something similar but far less serious and bullying with me. Those who know him say Agnew is a very nice man, that he can just be thin-skinned. This is probably right."
Several months later, in August 2019, Agnew made a number of public tweets about Liew, calling him a "sad racist troll after clickbait” and a "horrible man". Agnew deleted his Twitter account after Liew made a formal complaint to the BBC about the tweets.

spans.name: cricket_stats
spans.input: What is Royal Challengers Bangalore's highest total against Chennai Super Kings in IPL matches?
spans.output: Royal Challengers Bangalore's highest total against Chennai Super Kings in IPL matches is 250 runs.

steps.name: sqlgen #1
steps.output:
SELECT MAX(i.runs) AS highest_total
FROM cricsheet.innings i
JOIN cricsheet.matches m ON m.match_id = i.match_id
WHERE m.format = 'IPL'
  AND i.batting_team ILIKE '%Royal Challengers%'
  AND i.bowling_team ILIKE '%Chennai%'
  AND (
    (m.team1 ILIKE '%Royal Challengers%' AND m.team2 ILIKE '%Chennai%')
    OR (m.team1 ILIKE '%Chennai%' AND m.team2 ILIKE '%Royal Challengers%')
  )
steps.input:
[system]
Write ONE read-only PostgreSQL SELECT to answer a cricket question over this schema:
cricsheet — 4 tables. Row granularity and column meaning both matter; read them before writing SQL.

matches — one row per match
  match_id                     primary key
  format                       'Test' | 'ODI' | 'T20I' | 'IPL'
  season                       e.g. '2023' or '2007/08'
  event_name                   series / tournament, e.g. 'India tour of England', 'ICC World Cup'
  match_date                   date
  venue, city                  ground and city
  team1, team2                 the two sides, full names (e.g. 'New Zealand')
  toss_winner, toss_decision
  winner                       winning team; NULL = tie / draw / no result
  result                       'normal' | 'tie' | 'draw' | 'no result'
  win_by_runs, win_by_wickets  victory margin — only one is set, per how they won
  player_of_match

innings — one row per innings (usually two per limited-overs match)
  match_id, innings_no
  batting_team, bowling_team   BOTH live here (deliveries has batting_team only)
  runs                         the FULL innings total, already summed — NOT a per-ball value
  wickets                      wickets lost in the innings

deliveries — one row per ball bowled
  match_id, innings_no, over_no, ball_no    ball location
  batting_team                 (no bowling_team column here — it is on innings)
  batter, bowler, non_striker
  runs_batter                  runs off the bat on THIS ball (0-6). A batter's innings/match total is
                               SUM(runs_batter) GROUP BY (match_id, innings_no, batter). A six = 6.
  runs_extras                  extras conceded on this ball
  runs_total                   runs_batter + runs_extras on this ball
  extra_type                   'wides' | 'noballs' | 'byes' | 'legbyes' | NULL
  wicket_kind                  dismissal type if a wicket fell this ball, else NULL. A bowler is NOT
                               credited for 'run out','retired hurt','retired out','obstructing the field'.
  player_out                   the dismissed batter, else NULL

player_lineups — one row per player per match
  match_id, team, player

NOT in this database: who captained a match (there is NO captain column — player_lineups is who played,
not who led), coaches, and player biography/age. It records match events and player run/wicket/six
tallies, not roles or careers. Questions about captaincy, leadership, or a player's career/biography
cannot be answered here — do not fabricate them from lineups or matches played.

Names: players are stored as initial(s)+surname, e.g. 'DW Steyn', 'DL Vettori', 'MG Johnson'. Match on
the SURNAME with a leading wildcard — bowler ILIKE '%Steyn' — never exact '='. Never put the given
name inside the wildcards: '%Dale Steyn%' matches nothing, because no stored value carries the given
name, and worse, it can match an unrelated player who happens to be registered under a full name. A
surname alone can cover several players; when you cannot pin the initials, GROUP BY the name column and
return one row per player rather than summing strangers together.

Teams and events: international team names are stable (New Zealand, Sri Lanka), but franchise and event names
vary and get renamed — match those with ILIKE. A franchise can appear under more than one string across
its history: the city gets respelled, a sponsor is added or dropped, the nickname is kept. Both forms
sit in the table as different values, so a filter on the full registered name silently reads half the
club and the query still returns rows. Match on ONE distinctive word, whichever part of the name is
likely to have survived, never the whole name. format already
scopes the competition (IPL/ODI/Test/T20I), so do NOT also
filter on event_name for a single match — it over-constrains and can return nothing. To locate a
specific match ('the final', 'the match against X'), identify it by the teams and the relevant/latest
date; do NOT add a winner filter unless the question asks who won.

Phrasing: 'X runs off Y balls' is batting notation for one innings — X runs while facing Y balls. The
ball count DESCRIBES the innings; it is NOT a filter on ball_no or over_no. For a batter's runs in a
match, SUM(runs_batter) for that batter in that match (their balls faced, if asked, is COUNT(*)).

Series/tours: identified by (event_name, season), e.g. ('India tour of England','2025'); to answer who
won a series, aggregate that series' matches by the winner column (winner IS NULL = a draw). For the
'last/most recent' series between two teams, take the (event_name, season) with the latest match_date
where both teams appear, then tally winners.
Example — who won the last New Zealand-Bangladesh Test series:
  WITH s AS (SELECT event_name, season FROM cricsheet.matches WHERE format='Test'
    AND team1 IN ('New Zealand','Bangladesh') AND team2 IN ('New Zealand','Bangladesh')
    GROUP BY event_name, season ORDER BY MAX(match_date) DESC LIMIT 1)
  SELECT m.winner, COUNT(*) FROM cricsheet.matches m
    JOIN s ON m.event_name=s.event_name AND m.season=s.season GROUP BY m.winner;
This returns each team's win count plus a NULL-winner row = drawn Tests; do NOT filter out NULL winners.

Trophy names are NOT stored. Cricsheet labels most bilateral series as tours, so the 2024/25
Border-Gavaskar Trophy lives under event_name 'India tour of Australia'. Identify a bilateral series by
format + season + the two teams and leave event_name out of it — never filter event_name on a
colloquial trophy name (Border-Gavaskar, The Ashes, Freedom Trophy, Pataudi). event_name is for
multi-team tournaments ('ICC World Cup', 'Indian Premier League').

A split-year season is written with a slash — '2024/25', never '2024-25' or '2024-2025'. It is ONE
season value: filter `season = '2022/23'`, and NEVER expand it into two calendar years — not
`season IN ('2022','2023')`, not `EXTRACT(year FROM match_date) IN (2022,2023)`, not a
Jan-2022–Dec-2023 date range. Expanding it pulls a second season's matches, and in T20Is it lets
associate-nation players accumulate across both years and outrank the real leader. A bare year in a
question ('most wickets in Tests 2017', 'the IPL 2016') also names the season column, not the calendar
year — filter season, not EXTRACT(year FROM match_date). The two disagree: a season labelled '2017'
holds matches either side of the new year, and a tour that starts in December sits in '2024/25'.

Never mask an empty result. Do not wrap an aggregate in COALESCE(...,0) and do not add a fallback row:
when nothing matches, the query must come back empty. A zero conjured by COALESCE reads downstream as a
real answer — 'scored 0 runs' — and it stops the retry that would have fixed the filters.
Return ONLY the SQL — no markdown fences, no prose.

steps.name: execute #1
steps.input:
SELECT match_date, winner, win_by_runs, win_by_wickets
FROM cricsheet.matches
WHERE format = 'IPL'
  AND ((team1 ILIKE '%Challengers%' AND team2 ILIKE '%Chennai%')
       OR (team1 ILIKE '%Chennai%' AND team2 ILIKE '%Challengers%'))
  AND winner ILIKE '%Challengers%'
ORDER BY match_date DESC
LIMIT 1

likely to have survived, never the whole name. format already
scopes the competition (IPL/ODI/Test/T20I), so do NOT also
filter on event_name for a single match — it over-constrains and can return nothing. To locate a
specific match ('the final', 'the match against X'), identify it by the teams and the relevant/latest
date; do NOT add a winner filter unless the question asks who won.

Phrasing: 'X runs off Y balls' is batting notation for one innings — X runs while facing Y balls. The
ball count DESCRIBES the innings; it is NOT a filter on ball_no or over_no. For a batter's runs in a
match, SUM(runs_batter) for that batter in that match (their balls faced, if asked, is COUNT(*)).

Series/tours: identified by (event_name, season), e.g. ('India tour of England','2025'); to answer who
won a series, aggregate that series' matches by the winner column (winner IS NULL = a draw). For the
'last/most recent' series between two teams, take the (event_name, season) with the latest match_date
where both teams appear, then tally winners.
Example — who won the last New Zealand-Bangladesh Test series:
  WITH s AS (SELECT event_name, season FROM cricsheet.matches WHERE format='Test'
    AND team1 IN ('New Zealand','Bangladesh') AND team2 IN ('New Zealand','Bangladesh')
    GROUP BY event_name, season ORDER BY MAX(match_date) DESC LIMIT 1)
  SELECT m.winner, COUNT(*) FROM cricsheet.matches m
    JOIN s ON m.event_name=s.event_name AND m.season=s.season GROUP BY m.winner;
This returns each team's win count plus a NULL-winner row = drawn Tests; do NOT filter out NULL winners.

Trophy names are NOT stored. Cricsheet labels most bilateral series as tours, so the 2024/25
Border-Gavaskar Trophy lives under event_name 'India tour of Australia'. Identify a bilateral series by
format + season + the two teams and leave event_name out of it — never filter event_name on a
colloquial trophy name (Border-Gavaskar, The Ashes, Freedom Trophy, Pataudi). event_name is for
multi-team tournaments ('ICC World Cup', 'Indian Premier League').

A split-year season is written with a slash — '2024/25', never '2024-25' or '2024-2025'. It is ONE
season value: filter `season = '2022/23'`, and NEVER expand it into two calendar years — not
`season IN ('2022','2023')`, not `EXTRACT(year FROM match_date) IN (2022,2023)`, not a
Jan-2022–Dec-2023 date range. Expanding it pulls a second season's matches, and in T20Is it lets
associate-nation players accumulate across both years and outrank the real leader. A bare year in a
question ('most wickets in Tests 2017', 'the IPL 2016') also names the season column, not the calendar
year — filter season, not EXTRACT(year FROM match_date). The two disagree: a season labelled '2017'
holds matches either side of the new year, and a tour that starts in December sits in '2024/25'.

Never mask an empty result. Do not wrap an aggregate in COALESCE(...,0) and do not add a fallback row:
when nothing matches, the query must come back empty. A zero conjured by COALESCE reads downstream as a
real answer — 'scored 0 runs' — and it stops the retry that would have fixed the filters.
Return ONLY the SQL — no markdown fences, no prose.

spans.input: What types of pitches are allowed in T20I matches, and can a hybrid pitch be used?
spans.output: In T20I matches, the standard requirement is that matches be played on natural turf pitches, though the addendum updates this to allow natural turf **or artificial pitches**. In either case, a hybrid pitch is permitted as an alternate, but only with the prior approval of the ICC. Additionally, the use of PVA and other adhesives in the preparation of (turf) pitches is not permitted.

answer:
Based on the official ICC playing conditions for T20Is (via cricket_rules):

- **Standard requirement:** T20I matches are to be played on **natural turf pitches**.
- **Addendum update:** This has been expanded to also allow **artificial pitches**.
- **Hybrid pitches:** Yes — a **hybrid pitch is permitted** as an alternate to natural turf or artificial pitches, but **only with the prior approval of the ICC**.
- **Additional restriction:** The use of PVA and other adhesives in the preparation of turf pitches is **not permitted**.

So to directly answer your question: yes, a hybrid pitch can be used in T20Is, provided the ICC has given its prior approval.

Question: What are the umpires responsible for checking about match conduct, equipment, fair play, and whether play is safe, before the toss and during the game?
steps.output:
Based on the ICC playing conditions, before the toss and during the match, the umpires must satisfy themselves of the following:

- **Conduct of the match, implements and equipment**: That the match is conducted strictly in accordance with the Playing Conditions; that implements conform to requirements regarding the ball (clause 4), the externally visible requirements of the bat (clause 5 and Appendix B paragraph 1), and the stumps and bails (clauses 8.2 and 8.3); that no player uses equipment other than that permitted (per Appendix A paragraph 2, noting the interpretation of "protective helmet"); and that the wicket-keeper's gloves comply with clause 27.2.

- **Fair and unfair play**: The umpires are the sole judges of fair and unfair play.

- **Fitness for play**: The umpires together must decide whether conditions of ground, weather, light, or exceptional circumstances make it dangerous or unreasonable for play to take place. Conditions are not considered dangerous or unreasonable merely because they are not ideal (e.g., wet grass or a wet ball alone does not make conditions unreasonable). Conditions are regarded as dangerous only if there is an actual and foreseeable risk to the safety of any player or umpire.

answer:
According to the ICC playing conditions (via **cricket_rules**), before the toss and throughout the match, the umpires must satisfy themselves of four main things:

1. **Conduct of the match, implements and equipment** – that the game is being played strictly per the Playing Conditions, and that the ball, bat (externally visible features), stumps, bails, and wicket-keeper's gloves all meet the required specifications; also that no player uses any equipment not permitted (e.g., unauthorised protective gear).

2. **Fair and unfair play** – the umpires act as the sole judges of what constitutes fair vs. unfair play throughout the game.

3. **Fitness of ground, weather and light** – the two umpires jointly decide whether conditions (ground, weather, light) or any exceptional circumstances make it dangerous or unreasonable for play to continue or start.

4. **Standard for "dangerous or unreasonable"** – conditions aren't deemed dangerous or unreasonable just because they're less than ideal (e.g., a wet outfield or damp ball alone isn't enough). They are only classified as dangerous if there's an **actual and foreseeable risk to the safety of any player or umpire**.

In short, umpires check legality of gear, ensure fairness of play, and jointly assess real safety risk (not mere imperfection) before and during play.

Question: What happens if a bowler is unable to finish an over because they become incapacitated or are suspended during it?

Another bowler will then complete the over from the same end, provided that this replacement bowler does not end up bowling two overs consecutively, nor bowl parts of each of two consecutive overs, in that innings.

answer:
According to the MCC Laws of Cricket (via **cricket_rules**):

If a bowler becomes **incapacitated** (e.g., injured) or is **suspended** while delivering an over — even while running up to bowl the very first ball of that over — the umpire must **call and signal Dead ball**.

Another bowler is then brought in to **complete the unfinished over from the same end**. The only restriction is that this replacement bowler must not, as a result, end up bowling **two overs in succession**, or bowl **parts of each of two consecutive overs** in that innings — the normal rule against a bowler delivering consecutive overs still applies.

Question: What happens if an umpire suspects a bowler has thrown the ball (illegal delivery/throwing) for the first time, and what happens if the same bowler repeats it later in the same innings? Also what are the rules regarding underarm bowling?
steps.output:
Under the ICC ODI and T20I playing conditions (Law 21.3):

**First occurrence:** If either umpire believes a ball has been thrown (a delivery not corresponding to the bowler's normal bowling action) or delivered underarm, the umpire calls and signals No ball. Once the ball is dead, the umpire informs the other umpire of the reason. The bowler's end umpire then gives the bowler a first and final warning (applicable for the rest of that innings), informs the fielding captain of the reason, and informs the batters at the wicket of what occurred.

**Repeat occurrence in the same innings:** If either umpire considers a further delivery by the same bowler is thrown or delivered underarm, the umpire again calls and signals No ball and informs the other umpire once the ball is dead. The bowler's end umpire then directs the fielding captain to suspend the bowler immediately from bowling for the rest of that innings. If the over is incomplete, another bowler must finish it—one who did not bowl the previous over (or part of it) and who will not be allowed to bowl any part of the next over. The umpire also informs the batters and, as soon as practicable, the batting side's captain of the reason for this action.

**Underarm bowling:** The rules treat underarm deliveries the same way as throwing—both are covered under the same clause. A suspected underarm delivery triggers the same No ball call, warning process on first occurrence, and suspension of the bowler on a repeat occurrence in that innings.

answer:
**Source: cricket_rules**

Under the ICC playing conditions (Law 21.3, unfair bowling — throwing/underarm), both offenses are treated identically:

**First time in the innings:**
- The umpire calls and signals **No ball**.
- Once the ball is dead, the bowler's-end umpire tells the other umpire the reason, gives the bowler a **first and final warning** (valid for the rest of that innings), informs the fielding captain why, and tells the batters what happened.

**If the same bowler does it again later in the same innings:**
- The umpire again calls and signals **No ball**.
- Once the ball is dead, the bowler's-end umpire tells the other umpire, and then directs the fielding captain to **immediately suspend that bowler from bowling for the rest of the innings**.
- If the bowler's over is unfinished, it must be completed by another bowler — one who did not bowl the previous over (or part of it) and who also won't be allowed to bowl the next over.
- The umpire informs the batters, and as soon as practical, the batting captain, of the reason for the suspension.

So essentially: **warning on the first offense, permanent ban from bowling for that innings on the second offense** — the same process applies whether the illegal action is a thrown delivery or an underarm delivery.

Under MCC Laws, this report goes to the Executive of the offending side and any Governing Body responsible for the match, who shall take appropriate action against the captain, other individuals involved, and the team if necessary (Law 27.4.2). Under ICC playing conditions, the equivalent report goes to the ICC Match Referee, who shall take action as considered appropriate.

27.4.2  In the event of unfair movement by the wicket-keeper, either 
umpire shall call and signal Dead ball and inform the other 
umpire of the reason for doing so. The bowler’s end umpire 
shall then:

27.4.2.1  award the one-run penalty for Wide or No ball, 
if applicable

27.4.2.2  award 5 Penalty runs to the batting side

27.4.2.3  inform the captain of the fielding side of the reason 
for this action

27.4.2.4  inform the batters and, as soon as practicable, the 
captain of the batting side of what has occurred.
The umpires together shall report the occurrence as soon 
as possible after the match to the Executive of the offending 
side and to any Governing Body responsible for the match, 
who shall take such action as is considered appropriate 
against the captain, any other individuals concerned and, if 
appropriate, the team.

27.5  Restriction on actions of wicket-keeper
If, in the opinion of either umpire, the wicket-keeper 
interferes with the striker’s right to play the ball, Law

20.4.2.6 (Umpire calling and signalling Dead ball) shall apply. 
However, if the wicket-keeper, or any other fielder, interferes 
with a legal second strike, either umpire shall call and signal 
Dead ball and the striker will not be dismissed, but the ball 
shall count as one for the over.
If, however, either umpire considers that the interference 
by the wicket-keeper was wilful, then Law 41.4 (Deliberate 
attempt to distract or obstruct striker) shall also apply.

Question: What must the umpires do after the match if the wicket-keeper has been involved in an incident of unfair play or contravention (e.g., illegal movement/gloves)?
steps.output: Based on the context, if the wicket-keeper is involved in unfair movement, the umpires together must report the occurrence as soon as possible after the match. Under MCC Laws, this report goes to the Executive of the offending side and any Governing Body responsible for the match, who shall take appropriate action against the captain, other individuals involved, and the team if necessary (Law 27.4.2). Under ICC playing conditions, the equivalent report goes to the ICC Match Referee, who shall take action as considered appropriate.

answer:
Per the MCC Laws / ICC playing conditions (cricket_rules):

If the wicket-keeper has been involved in such an incident (e.g., illegal movement or contravention of the wicket-keeper's restrictions), the umpires must **jointly report the occurrence as soon as possible after the match**.

- **Under MCC Laws (Law 27.4.2):** the report goes to the **Executive of the offending side** and to **any Governing Body responsible for the match**, who are then responsible for taking appropriate action against the captain, any other individuals involved, and the team if necessary.
- **Under ICC Playing Conditions:** the equivalent report is made to the **ICC Match Referee**, who takes whatever action is considered appropriate.

So in short: the umpires don't penalize on the spot for this — they file a post-match report to the relevant authority (team management/governing body, or the match referee in international cricket), which then decides on any further action.

Context:

31.5 Answering appeals
The striker’s end umpire shall answer all appeals arising out of any of clauses 35  
(Hit wicket), 39 (Stumped) or 38 (Run out) when this occurs at the wicket-keeper’s end. 
The bowler’s end umpire shall answer all other appeals.
When an appeal is made, each umpire shall answer on any matter that falls within  
his jurisdiction.
When a batter has been given Not out, either umpire may answer an appeal, made in 
accordance with clause 31.3, if it is on a further matter and is within his jurisdiction.

31.6 Consultation by umpires
Each umpire shall answer appeals on matters within his/her own jurisdiction. If an umpire 
is doubtful about any point that the other umpire may have been in a better position to 
see, he/she shall consult the latter on this point of fact and shall then give the decision. 
If, after consultation, there is still doubt remaining, the decision shall be Not out.

31.7 Batter leaving the wicket under a misapprehension
An umpire shall intervene if satisfied that a batter, not having been given out, has left 
the wicket under a misapprehension of being out. The umpire intervening shall call and 
signal Dead ball to prevent any further action by the fielding side and shall recall the 
batter.
A batter may be recalled at any time up to the instant when the ball comes into play for 
the next delivery, unless it is the final wicket of the innings, in which case it should be up 
to the instant when the umpires leave the field.

38.3  Non-striker leaving their ground early

38.3.1  If the non-striker is out of their ground at any time from the  
 moment the ball comes into play until the moment the  
 bowler’s arm reaches the highest point of their normal   
 bowling action in the delivery swing,  the non-striker is liable  
 to be Run out. In these circumstances, the non-striker will  
 be out Run out if they are out of their ground when the  
 wicket is broken by the bowler throwing the ball at the   
 wicket or by the bowler’s hand holding the ball, whether 
 or not the ball is subsequently delivered.

38.3.1.1  Even if the non-striker had left their ground before the 
moment the bowler’s arm reaches the highest point 
of their normal bowling action in the delivery swing, 
once the bowler has reached that point it is no longer 
possible for the bowler to run out the non-striker 
under this Law.

38.3.2  If the ball is not delivered,
    if there is an appeal, the umpire shall make their decision 
on the Run out. 
    if there is no appeal, or if the decision is not out, the 
umpire shall call and signal Dead ball as soon as possible.
    the ball shall not count as one in the over.

Question: When can a bowler run out the non-striker for leaving their ground before the ball is released? (Mankad rule)
steps.output: A bowler can run out the non-striker if the non-striker is out of their ground at any point from the moment the ball comes into play until the moment the bowler’s arm reaches the highest point of their normal bowling action in the delivery swing (i.e., the instant the bowler would normally be expected to release the ball). This applies whether the wicket is broken by the bowler throwing the ball at it or by the bowler's hand while still holding the ball, regardless of whether the ball is subsequently delivered. However, once the bowler's arm passes that highest point in the delivery swing, it is no longer possible to run out the non-striker under this law, even if they had left their ground earlier.

answer:
**Answer (from MCC Laws via cricket_rules):**

A bowler is permitted to run out the non-striker (the so-called "Mankad" dismissal) if the non-striker is out of their ground **at any point from when the ball comes into play until the instant the bowler's arm reaches the highest point of their normal bowling action** (i.e., the moment they would normally be expected to release the ball).

Key details:
- This applies whether the wicket is broken by the bowler throwing the ball at the stumps, or with the ball still in hand — it doesn't matter if the ball is actually delivered afterward.
- **Once the bowler's arm passes that highest point** in the delivery swing, the window closes — the bowler can no longer run out the non-striker under this law, even if the batter had left their ground earlier in the action.

So essentially, the legal window is: from the ball coming into play up to (but not beyond) the top of the bowler's delivery swing.

Question: What must the umpire do if a bowler is found to have deliberately overstepped the front foot line for a no ball?
steps.output:
If the umpire considers that a bowler has delivered a deliberate front-foot No ball, the umpire must:

- Immediately call and signal No ball.
- When the ball is dead, direct the captain of the fielding side to suspend the bowler immediately from bowling, and inform the other umpire of the reason for this action.
- Ensure that, if applicable, the over is completed by another bowler—one who did not bowl any part of the previous over and will not be allowed to bowl any part of the next over.
- Inform the batters, and as soon as practicable, the captain of the batting side.

The suspended bowler is not allowed to bowl again in that innings (per the ICC playing conditions) or, per the MCC Laws, for the remainder of the match. The umpires must also report the occurrence: under the ICC conditions, together to the ICC Match Referee, who will take appropriate action; under the MCC Laws, to the Executive of the offending side and any Governing Body responsible for the match, who shall take appropriate action.

answer:
Per the official rules (MCC Laws / ICC playing conditions):

If an umpire believes a bowler has deliberately overstepped the front-foot line to deliver a no ball, the umpire must:

1. **Immediately call and signal No ball.**
2. **Once the ball is dead**, direct the fielding captain to **suspend the bowler from bowling immediately**, and inform the other umpire of the reason.
3. **Arrange for another bowler** to complete the over — one who did not bowl any part of the previous over and will not bowl in the next over either.
4. **Inform the batters**, and as soon as practicable, inform the **captain of the batting side**.

Consequences for the bowler: under **ICC playing conditions**, the bowler cannot bowl again in that innings; under the **MCC Laws**, the ban extends to the **rest of the match**.

Finally, the umpires must **report the incident** — jointly to the **ICC Match Referee** (who takes appropriate action) under ICC conditions, or to the **Executive of the offending side and the relevant Governing Body** under the MCC Laws.

spans.name: cricket_rules
spans.input: What parts are counted as part of a batter's bat, and what counts as a hand holding the bat?
spans.output:
Based on the provided rules, the following are considered part of the bat:

1. The whole of the bat itself.
2. The whole of any glove(s) worn on the hand(s) holding the bat.
3. The hand(s) holding the bat, if the batter is not wearing a glove on that hand or those hands.

As for what counts as a hand "holding" the bat: contact between a batter's hand (or a glove worn on their hand) and any part of the bat constitutes the bat being held in that hand.

Question: What parts are counted as part of a batter's bat, and what counts as a hand holding the bat?
steps.output:
Based on the provided rules, the following are considered part of the bat:

1. The whole of the bat itself.
2. The whole of any glove(s) worn on the hand(s) holding the bat.
3. The hand(s) holding the bat, if the batter is not wearing a glove on that hand or those hands.

As for what counts as a hand "holding" the bat: contact between a batter's hand (or a glove worn on their hand) and any part of the bat constitutes the bat being held in that hand.

answer:
Per the MCC Laws of Cricket, as clarified by the rules corpus:

**What counts as part of the bat:**
1. The bat itself (the whole implement).
2. Any glove(s) worn on the hand(s) that are holding the bat — the entire glove counts as part of the bat.
3. The bare hand(s) holding the bat, if no glove is worn on that hand — in that case the hand itself is treated as part of the bat.

**What counts as a hand "holding" the bat:**
A hand is considered to be holding the bat whenever there is contact between that hand (or a glove worn on it) and any part of the bat. So as long as the hand/glove is touching the bat in some way, it's deemed to be "holding" it for the purposes of these provisions.

This matters practically for decisions like caught, lbw, and handled-the-ball type situations, since the bat and the hand(s) holding it are treated as a single unit under the Laws.

(Source: cricket_rules — MCC Laws)

answer:
According to the official playing conditions (ICC T20I/ODI/Test and IPL rules, clause 3.6.5): **yes**, the review is still deemed **Unsuccessful**.

The key factor is the *original on-field decision*, not the specific mode of dismissal. If the on-field call was "Out" and the third umpire's review confirms the batter is out — even if the actual mode of dismissal turns out to be different from what was originally given (e.g., given out LBW but actually caught, or given out caught but actually bowled) — the on-field decision itself stands unchanged. Since the review didn't overturn the umpire's call, it counts as Unsuccessful and is deducted from the reviewing team's limit of reviews for that innings.

Source: cricket_rules (ICC/IPL playing conditions).

answer:
Yes — according to the ICC (Test/ODI/T20I) and IPL playing conditions, the match referee has this authority. When approving a concussion replacement, the referee's role is to ensure it's a genuine "like-for-like" swap for the concussed player. If the referee judges that the nominated replacement, in carrying out their normal playing role, would give the team an excessive/unfair advantage, the referee can impose whatever conditions they see fit on the identity and involvement of that replacement — for example, restricting the role the replacement is allowed to play in the match — to keep things fair rather than letting the substitution become a de facto tactical upgrade.

(Source: cricket_rules)

spans.input: What happens if the team fielding second has not completed 20 overs by the scheduled end time in a T20 match?
spans.output: If the team fielding second fails to bowl its 20 overs by the scheduled cessation time, play is extended — the hours of play shall be extended until the required number of overs has been bowled or a result is achieved. Penalties for slow over rates will also apply, as provided under the ICC Code of Conduct (clause 13.8).

answer: Under the playing conditions (per **cricket_rules**), if the side bowling second in a T20 hasn't completed its 20 overs by the scheduled finishing time, play isn't simply cut off — the hours of play are **extended** until either that required number of overs is bowled or a result is reached. On top of that, the fielding side faces **slow over-rate penalties** under the ICC Code of Conduct (clause 13.8), separate from the extension of playing time.

spans.output: passed

spans.name: cricket_rules
spans.input: If the second innings is interrupted after at least five overs have been bowled and the match cannot be restarted, how is the winner decided using the DLS par score?
spans.output:
Based on the ICC T20I and IPL playing conditions, if the second innings is suspended after at least 5 overs have been bowled and the match cannot resume, the result is determined by comparing the batting side's score to the DLS 'Par Score' calculated at the moment of suspension:

- If the score equals the par score, the match is a **Tie**.
- If the score exceeds the par score, the batting side wins by the margin of runs above the par score.
- If the score falls short of the par score, the batting side loses by the margin of runs below the par score.

determined at the instant of the suspension using DLS. If the score 
is equal to the par score, the match is a Tie. Otherwise the result is a 
victory, or defeat, by the margin of runs by which the score exceeds, or 
falls short of, the Par Score.

16.5 Winning hit or extras

16.5.1 As soon as a result is reached as defined in clauses 16.1, 16.2 or 16.3.1, the match 
is at an end. Nothing that happens thereafter, except as in clause 41.17 (Penalty 
runs), shall be regarded as part of it. Note also clause 16.8.

16.5.2 The side batting last will have scored enough runs to win only if its total of runs 
is sufficient without including any runs completed by the batters before the 
completion of a catch, or the obstruction of a catch, from which the striker could 
be dismissed.

16.5.3 If a boundary is scored before the batters have completed sufficient runs to win 
the match, the whole of the boundary allowance shall be credited to the side’s 
total and, in the case of a hit by the bat, to the striker’s score.

Question: If the second innings is interrupted after at least five overs have been bowled and the match cannot be restarted, how is the winner decided using the DLS par score?
steps.output:
Based on the ICC T20I and IPL playing conditions, if the second innings is suspended after at least 5 overs have been bowled and the match cannot resume, the result is determined by comparing the batting side's score to the DLS 'Par Score' calculated at the moment of suspension:

- If the score equals the par score, the match is a **Tie**.
- If the score exceeds the par score, the batting side wins by the margin of runs above the par score.
- If the score falls short of the par score, the batting side loses by the margin of runs below the par score.

answer:
**Answer (from ICC T20I/IPL playing conditions, per cricket_rules):**

Once at least 5 overs have been bowled in the second innings and the match cannot be resumed, the result is decided by comparing the chasing team's actual score at the point of interruption to the DLS "Par Score" for that number of overs/wickets:

- **Score = Par Score** → Match is a **Tie**.
- **Score > Par Score** → The batting (chasing) side **wins**, by a margin equal to the runs scored above the par score.
- **Score < Par Score** → The batting (chasing) side **loses**, by a margin equal to the runs short of the par score.

This is the standard mechanism used to determine winners in rain-affected limited-overs matches under the Duckworth-Lewis-Stern method.

- The one-run penalty for a No ball is scored as a No ball extra.

steps.output:
When the ball is struck by the bat, all runs scored by the batting side are credited to the striker personally (not just to the team total in a general sense—he is the one recorded as scoring them).

However, two exceptions are treated differently as extras rather than being credited to the striker:
- An award of 5 Penalty runs is scored as Penalty runs.
- The one-run penalty for a No ball is scored as a No ball extra.

So even though the ball was hit by the bat, these specific runs (the 5-run penalty and the one-run No ball penalty) are not credited to the batter's individual score—they go down as extras/penalty runs instead.

answer:
According to the MCC Laws (via cricket_rules):

- **When the ball is hit by the bat**, all runs scored off that delivery are credited personally to the striker (the batter), not just added anonymously to the team total.

- **Exceptions – treated differently:**
  - **Penalty runs (5-run awards)** are recorded as *Penalty runs*, not credited to the batter, even if the ball was struck.
  - **The one-run penalty for a No ball** is recorded as a *No ball extra*, again not added to the batter's individual score.

So even though contact with the bat is what produces the runs on that ball, these two categories are always logged as extras/penalties rather than boosting the batter's personal tally.

spans.input: When an umpire has to call Dead ball because of unfair play or a serious injury, when is the ball considered dead - i.e., from what moment does the dead ball take effect?
spans.output: Based on Law/Clause 20.4.2, when an umpire calls and signals Dead ball due to unfair play (20.4.2.1) or a possibly serious injury to a player or umpire (20.4.2.2), the ball is considered to be dead **at the instant of the incident** that caused it to become dead — not merely from the moment the umpire actually calls and signals it. This is distinct from certain other specified situations (such as unfair actions or unacceptable conduct) where the Playing Conditions/Law allow the call to be delayed, in which case the ball is only considered dead at the point of the call.

steps.output: Based on Law/Clause 20.4.2, when an umpire calls and signals Dead ball due to unfair play (20.4.2.1) or a possibly serious injury to a player or umpire (20.4.2.2), the ball is considered to be dead **at the instant of the incident** that caused it to become dead — not merely from the moment the umpire actually calls and signals it. This is distinct from certain other specified situations (such as unfair actions or unacceptable conduct) where the Playing Conditions/Law allow the call to be delayed, in which case the ball is only considered dead at the point of the call.

answer:
According to the MCC Laws (Law 20.4.2), when an umpire calls and signals **Dead ball** because of:

- **unfair play** (Law 20.4.2.1), or
- **a serious injury** to a player, umpire, or other participant (Law 20.4.2.2)

the ball is deemed to have become dead **at the instant of the incident** that caused the umpire to make the call — not merely from the moment the umpire actually calls and signals "Dead ball."

This is an important distinction: in these two specific cases (unfair play and serious injury), the dead-ball status is retroactive to the triggering event, even though the umpire's call may come a moment later. This differs from certain other situations (e.g., some cases of unfair action or player conduct) where the Laws/Playing Conditions specify that the ball only becomes dead at the actual point the umpire makes the call, allowing any play up to that point to stand.

*Source: cricket_rules (MCC Laws, Law 20.4.2)*

Yes — under the Laws of Cricket, a batter who retires because of illness, injury, or any other unavoidable cause is entitled to come back and resume their innings later, but they can only do so at the fall of a wicket or when another batter retires (they can't just walk back in mid-partnership).

If the batter is unable to resume their innings for any reason, their innings is recorded as **"Retired – not out"** — it does not count as a dismissal against the bowler, and it doesn't affect the batter's not-out status.

(Source: cricket_rules)

- Award the one-run penalty for Wide or No ball, if applicable
- Award 5 Penalty runs to the batting side
- Inform the captain of the fielding side of the reason for this action
- Inform the batters and, as soon as practicable, the captain of the batting side of what has occurred

The umpires together shall also report the occurrence as soon as possible after the match to the ICC Match Referee, who will take appropriate action.

answer:
Per the ICC playing conditions (Law/Clause 28.6.3), if a fielder (other than the wicket-keeper) moves unfairly while the bowler is running in or during his/her delivery to try to distract the striker, the umpires must:

1. **Call and signal Dead ball** immediately (either umpire can do this) and inform the other umpire of the reason.
2. **Award 5 penalty runs** to the batting side.
3. Award any applicable **Wide or No-ball penalty** as well, if the movement coincided with an illegal delivery.
4. **Inform the fielding captain** of the reason for the action.
5. **Inform the batters**, and as soon as practicable, the **batting captain**, of what occurred.
6. **Report the incident** to the Match Referee after the match, who will take further appropriate action.

This is designed to penalize distracting or unfair fielding movements and ensure transparency for both sides.

**Source:** cricket_rules (ICC Test Match Playing Conditions, clause 28.6.3).

Question: In what situations is the striker not out hit wicket even though their wicket is put down?
steps.output:
Based on the provided rules (which are consistent across the ICC ODI, T20I, and Test playing conditions, as well as the MCC Laws), the striker is **not out** Hit Wicket if their wicket is put down in any of the following situations:

- It occurs **after** the striker has completed any action in receiving the delivery (other than setting off for the first run or making a further lawful stroke to guard the wicket).
- It occurs while the striker is **in the act of running**, other than when setting off immediately for the first run.
- It occurs when the striker is **trying to avoid being run out or stumped**.
- It occurs when the striker is **trying to avoid a throw-in** at any time.
- The **bowler does not deliver the ball** after entering the delivery stride (in which case the umpire calls and signals Dead ball).
- The delivery is a **No ball**.

Additionally, the MCC Laws specify two further situations where the striker is not out:
- The wicket is broken by a part of the striker’s **detached equipment or bat** (not held in the hand) that has come into contact with another person before breaking the wicket.
- The wicket is broken by the striker’s person or bat (held in the hand) as a **direct consequence of contact with a fielder**.

answer:
According to the official Laws/playing conditions (via cricket_rules), the striker is **not out Hit Wicket** in these situations, even if their wicket is put down:

1. **After completing the action of receiving the delivery** — i.e., once they've finished playing the ball, deliberately or not, the wicket being broken afterward doesn't count (except when setting off for the first run or making a further lawful stroke to guard the wicket).
2. **While in the act of running** — apart from the moment of setting off immediately for the first run.
3. **While trying to avoid being run out or stumped**.
4. **While trying to avoid a throw-in** at any time.
5. **If the bowler doesn't deliver the ball** after entering the delivery stride — the umpire calls Dead Ball instead.
6. **If the delivery is a No Ball**.

Two further protections under the MCC Laws:
7. The wicket is broken by **detached equipment or a bat not held in the hand**, after that item made contact with another person first.
8. The wicket is broken by the striker's **person or bat (held in hand)** as a **direct consequence of contact with a fielder**.

Source: cricket_rules (MCC Laws + ICC playing conditions).

Question: What happens if the same bowler is no-balled a third time in the innings for bowling too many fast short-pitched deliveries in an over?
steps.output: If the same bowler is no-balled a third time in the innings for bowling too many fast short-pitched deliveries in an over, the umpire shall call and signal No ball, and once the ball is dead, direct the fielding captain to suspend the bowler immediately from bowling for the remainder of that innings, while informing the other umpire of the reason. The over must then be completed by another bowler who neither bowled part of the previous over nor may bowl part of the next over. The umpire also informs the batters and, as soon as practicable, the batting captain, and the umpires may report the matter to the Match Referee for further action against the bowler.

answer:
Per the MCC Laws (Law 41.6, short-pitched fast bowling), here's what happens on the **third** such no-ball by the same bowler in an innings:

1. **The delivery is called and signalled No ball** as usual.
2. **Once the ball is dead**, the umpire directs the fielding captain that the offending bowler must be **suspended immediately from bowling for the rest of that innings** — this is an outright ban, not just a warning.
3. The umpire **informs the other umpire** of the reason for the suspension.
4. The **over is completed by a different bowler**, who must not have bowled the immediately preceding part of that over and is also barred from bowling the next over.
5. The **batters on strike, and as soon as practicable the batting captain**, are informed of the situation.
6. The umpires **may report the incident to the Match Referee**, who can take further disciplinary action against the bowler.

So in short: it's not just another no-ball penalty — the third offense in an innings ends that bowler's involvement in bowling for the remainder of the innings.

41.11 The protected area
The protected area is defined as that area of the pitch contained within a rectangle 
bounded at each end by imaginary lines parallel to the popping creases and 5 ft/1.52 m

joining the centres of the two middle stumps, each parallel to it and 1 ft/30.48 cm from it.

41.12 Fielder damaging the pitch

41.12.1 It is unfair to cause deliberate or avoidable damage to the pitch. A fielder will 
be deemed to be causing avoidable damage if either umpire considers that his 
presence on the pitch is without reasonable cause.

41.12.2 If a fielder causes deliberate or avoidable damage to the pitch, other than  
as in clause 41.13.1, at the first instance the umpire seeing the contravention  
shall, when the ball is dead, inform the other umpire. The bowler’s end umpire 
shall then
 –  caution the captain of the fielding side and indicate that this is a first and final 
warning. This warning shall apply throughout the innings.
 – inform the batters of what has occurred.

6 THE PITCH

6.1 Area of pitch
The pitch is a rectangular area of the ground 22 yards/20.12 m in length and  
10 ft/3.05 m in width. It is bounded at either end by the bowling creases and on either 
side by imaginary lines, one each side of the imaginary line joining the centres of the 
two middle stumps, each parallel to it and 5 ft/1.52 m from it. If the pitch is next to an 
artificial pitch which is closer than 5 ft/1.52 m from the middle stumps, the pitch on that 
side will extend only to the junction of the two surfaces. See clauses 8.1 (Description, 
width and pitching), 7.2 (The bowling crease) and Appendix C.

6.2 Fitness of pitch for play
The umpires shall be the sole judges of the fitness of the pitch for play. See clauses 2.7 
(Fitness for play) and 2.8 (Suspension of play in dangerous or unreasonable conditions).

6.3 Selection and preparation
Before the match, the Ground Authority shall be responsible for the selection and 
preparation of the pitch. During the match, the umpires shall control its use and 
maintenance.

6.3.1 The Ground Authority shall ensure that during the period prior to the start 
of play and during intervals, the pitch area shall be roped off so as to prevent 
unauthorised access. (The pitch area shall include an area at least 2 metres 
beyond the rectangle made by the crease markings at both ends of the pitch).

answer:
According to the MCC Laws of Cricket, the **protected area** of the pitch is defined as a rectangular zone:

- **Length-wise**: bounded at each end by imaginary lines parallel to the popping creases, extending **5 ft (1.52 m)** in from each popping crease.
- **Width-wise**: bounded on each side by imaginary lines running parallel to (and **1 ft / 30.48 cm** from) the line joining the centres of the two middle stumps at each end.

This central strip of the pitch is given extra protection primarily to prevent bowlers from causing excessive damage to it by running on it during their follow-through (which is regulated under the Laws), as this area is most crucial for the ball's bounce and behavior throughout the match.

*Source: cricket_rules (MCC Laws of Cricket)*

spans.input: Before deciding whether to ask for a Player Review, who is allowed to talk to whom, and under what limited circumstance may someone ask the bowler’s end umpire about an LBW decision?
spans.output: passed

spans.name: cricket_rules
spans.input: Before deciding whether to ask for a Player Review (DRS), who is allowed to talk to whom, and under what limited circumstance may a player ask the bowler's end umpire about an LBW decision?
spans.output:
Before deciding whether to request a Player Review, the captain may consult with the bowler and other fielders, and the two batters may consult with each other.

The only circumstance in which a player may query the umpire before making that decision is for an LBW appeal: either the captain or the two batters may ask the bowler's end umpire whether his/her decision was based on the striker having made no genuine attempt to play the ball with the bat. No other querying of the umpire is permitted at this stage.

3.3.1 On receipt of an eligible and timely request for a Player Review, the relevant

answer:
Based on the ICC/MCC playing conditions for the DRS (from **cricket_rules**):

- **Consultation allowed:** Before deciding whether to request a Player Review, the fielding captain may consult with the bowler and other fielders, and the two batters at the crease may consult with each other.
- **Limited query to the umpire:** The *only* thing anyone is permitted to ask the umpire at this stage concerns an LBW dismissal — either the fielding captain or the two batters may ask the bowler's end umpire whether the decision was based on the striker having made **no genuine attempt to play the ball with the bat**. No other question to the umpire is allowed before the review decision is made.

spans.name: input_guard
spans.input: What must the bowler’s front and back feet do during the delivery stride for the delivery to be fair?
spans.output: passed

spans.name: cricket_rules
spans.input: What must the bowler's front and back feet do during the delivery stride for the delivery to be fair (no-ball law)?
spans.output:
For a delivery to be fair with respect to the feet, during the delivery stride:

- **Back foot**: Must land within and not touching the return crease appertaining to the bowler's stated mode of delivery.
- **Front foot**: Must land with some part of the foot, whether grounded or raised, (1) on the same side of the imaginary line joining the two middle stumps as the return crease (as described for the back foot), and (2) behind the popping crease.

If either condition is not met, the bowler's end umpire (or third umpire, depending on the playing conditions) shall call and signal No ball.

Question: What must the bowler's front and back feet do during the delivery stride for the delivery to be fair (no-ball law)?
steps.output:
For a delivery to be fair with respect to the feet, during the delivery stride:

- **Back foot**: Must land within and not touching the return crease appertaining to the bowler's stated mode of delivery.
- **Front foot**: Must land with some part of the foot, whether grounded or raised, (1) on the same side of the imaginary line joining the two middle stumps as the return crease (as described for the back foot), and (2) behind the popping crease.

If either condition is not met, the bowler's end umpire (or third umpire, depending on the playing conditions) shall call and signal No ball.

answer:
According to the MCC Laws of Cricket, for a delivery to be fair with respect to the feet during the bowler's delivery stride:

- **Back foot** must land within, and not touching, the return crease on the appropriate side (matching the bowler's stated mode of delivery, i.e., over or round the wicket).
- **Front foot** must land with some part of the foot — whether grounded or raised — behind the popping crease, and on the correct side of the imaginary line joining the two middle stumps (the same side as the return crease used for the back foot).

If either requirement is not satisfied, the umpire at the bowler's end (or the third umpire, under relevant playing conditions) must call and signal **No ball**.

Source: cricket_rules (MCC Laws of Cricket).

Question: What must the umpire do if the same bowler breaks a rule (such as dangerous/unfair short-pitched or high full-pitched bowling) for a third time in an innings, when the ball is dead?
steps.output:
According to ICC ODI Playing Conditions (41.13.4), if the same bowler contravenes the relevant clause a third time in an innings, when the ball is dead the umpire shall:

- Direct the captain of the fielding side to suspend the bowler immediately from bowling. If applicable, the over must be completed by another bowler, who must not have bowled any part of the previous over nor be allowed to bowl any part of the next over. The suspended bowler is not allowed to bowl again in that innings.
- Inform the other umpire of the reason for this action.
- Inform the batters and, as soon as practicable, the captain of the batting side of what has occurred.

The umpires may then report the matter to the ICC Match Referee, who will take appropriate action against the bowler.

answer:
According to the ICC ODI Playing Conditions (Law/clause 41.13.4, unfair play — bowling), if the same bowler breaches the relevant rule (e.g., dangerous/unfair short-pitched or high full-pitched bowling) for a third time in an innings, when the ball becomes dead the umpire must:

1. **Direct the fielding captain to immediately suspend the bowler** from bowling for the remainder of that innings. If the bowler was mid-over, the over is completed by another bowler — one who hasn't bowled part of the previous over and won't bowl part of the next one.
2. **Inform the other umpire** of the reason for the action.
3. **Inform the batters**, and as soon as practicable the batting captain, of what has occurred.

The umpires can also report the incident to the Match Referee afterward, who may take further disciplinary action against the bowler.

Source: cricket_rules (ICC playing conditions).

It is solely for the umpires together to decide whether either conditions of ground, weather or light or exceptional circumstances mean that it would be dangerous or unreasonable for play to take place. Conditions shall not be regarded as either dangerous or unreasonable merely because they are not ideal. The fact that the grass and the ball are wet does not warrant the ground conditions being regarded as unreasonable or dangerous. 2.7.2 Conditions shall be regarded as dangerous if there is actual and foreseeable risk to the safety of any player or umpire. 2.7.3 Conditions shall be regarded as unreasonable if, although posing no risk to safety, it would not be sensible for play to proceed.

[icc_odi (p7)]
2.7.3 Conditions shall be regarded as unreasonable if, although posing no risk to 
safety, it would not be sensible for play to proceed.

2.7.4 If the umpires consider the ground is so wet or slippery as to deprive the bowler 
of a reasonable foothold, the fielders of the power of free movement, or the 
batters of the ability to play their strokes or to run between the wickets, then 
these conditions shall be regarded as so bad that it would be dangerous and 
unreasonable for play to take place.

2.8 Suspension of play in dangerous or unreasonable circumstances

2.8.1 All references to ground include the pitch. See clause 6.1 (Area of pitch).

2.8.2 If at any time the umpires together agree that the conditions of ground, weather 
or light, or any other circumstances are dangerous or unreasonable, they shall 
immediately suspend play, or not allow play to start or to recommence. The 
decision as to whether conditions are so bad as to warrant such action is one for 
the umpires alone to make, following consultation with the ICC Match Referee.

2.8.3 If circumstances are warranted, the umpires shall stop play and instruct the 
Ground Authority to take whatever action they can and use whatever equipment 
is necessary to remove as much dew as possible from the outfield when 
conditions become unreasonable or dangerous. The umpires may also instruct 
the ground staff to take such action during scheduled and unscheduled breaks  
in play.

[icc_t20i (p7)]
2.7.3 Conditions shall be regarded as unreasonable if, although posing no risk to

2.8.1 All references to ground include the pitch. See clause 6.1 (Area of pitch).

2.8.2 If at any time the umpires together agree that the conditions of ground, weather 
or light, or any other circumstances are dangerous or unreasonable, they shall 
immediately suspend play, or not allow play to start or to recommence. The 
decision as to whether conditions are so bad as to warrant such action is one for 
the umpires alone to make, following consultation with the ICC Match Referee.

2.8.3 If circumstances are warranted, the umpires shall stop play and instruct the 
Ground Authority to take whatever action they can and use whatever equipment 
is necessary to remove as much dew as possible from the outfield when 
conditions become unreasonable or dangerous. The umpires may also instruct 
the ground staff to take such action during scheduled and unscheduled breaks  
in play.

be used in accordance with these playing conditions.

2.8.5.1 All light meters shall be uniformly calibrated.

Question: When the ball becomes dead for a reason other than a wicket, which runs completed by the batting side still count, and how are runs credited if the batters had already crossed or completed a run at the point the ball became dead?
steps.output: When the ball becomes dead for any reason other than the fall of a wicket (or is called dead by an umpire), the batting side is credited with all runs completed by the batters before the incident or call of Dead ball, plus the run in progress if the batters had already crossed at the instant the ball became dead. Any penalty runs awarded to either side are also scored, subject to specific exceptions such as Leg byes not being awarded (clause 23.3) and provisions regarding protective helmets belonging to the fielding side (clause 28.3), as well as the rule on deliberate distraction, deception, or obstruction of a batter.

answer:
Per the MCC Laws (via cricket_rules):

When the ball becomes dead for any reason other than a wicket falling (or the umpire calling "dead ball"), the batting side keeps:

1. **All runs already completed** by the batters before the ball became dead.
2. **The run in progress**, *if and only if* the batters had already crossed each other at the instant the ball went dead — i.e., that additional run is credited as well.

So the key test for the "extra" run is whether the batters had crossed at the moment of dead ball: if they had, it counts; if not, it doesn't.

Additionally, any **penalty runs** awarded to either side are still added, with a few specific exceptions noted in the Laws — e.g., leg byes are not awarded in certain circumstances, and there are special provisions concerning a fielding side's protective helmet on the field, and cases of deliberate distraction, deception, or obstruction of a batter.
